title: Maharashtrian Zunka

subs.name: no direct substitute — the texture and flavour of zunka are entirely besan-dependent
2 large shallots for a slightly sweeter version, or omit for no-onion-no-garlic version adding extra asafoetida

steps.text: Dry-roast the besan in a kadai on low-medium heat for 3–4 minutes, stirring without stopping, until it smells nutty and toasty and turns a shade or two darker. Remove immediately to a plate — the besan should not colour beyond golden. This step removes the raw taste.

Heat oil in the same kadai on medium-high. Add mustard seeds; once they pop add cumin, asafoetida and curry leaves. Immediately add sliced garlic and cook for 30 seconds until just beginning to turn golden. Add onions, green chilli and ginger; sauté until the onion is soft and lightly golden, about 5 minutes.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder and coriander powder; stir for 20 seconds. Pour in 0.75 cup water and bring to a boil. Season with salt.

Lower the flame to medium and add the roasted besan to the boiling water in a thin, steady stream, whisking or stirring rapidly to prevent lumps. The mixture will thicken almost immediately and come together into a cohesive, crumbly mass as it cooks.

Reduce heat to low and cook for 3–4 minutes, stirring and scraping the bottom of the pan to prevent sticking, until the zunka is fully cooked, no longer raw-tasting, and has a crumbly but moist texture — it should look like very coarsely scrambled eggs. Scatter coriander leaves and serve hot with bhakri, chapati or plain rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

substitution_notes: besan → no direct substitute — the texture and flavour of zunka are entirely besan-dependent; onion → 2 large shallots for a slightly sweeter version, or omit for no-onion-no-garlic version adding extra asafoetida
